abstract: Neural machine translation (NMT) is a burgeoning approach to statistical translation that relies entirely on deep neural networks. Typically, these models utilize an encoder-decoder architecture: an encoder compresses a variable-length source sentence into a fixed-length vector, which a decoder then expands into a target translation. In this paper, we investigate the performance and limitations of NMT by comparing two specific architectures: the standard RNN Encoder-Decoder and a newly developed gated recursive convolutional neural network (grConv). Our analysis reveals that while NMT models perform competitively on short sentences with familiar vocabulary, their accuracy drops sharply as sentence length increases or when they encounter unknown words. Additionally, we demonstrate that the proposed grConv model is capable of automatically discovering and capturing the grammatical structure of sentences without explicit linguistic supervision.
